danger_signals.visual_form: Mechanical adversaries positioned throughout level spaces
danger_signals.meaning: Active hostile threats requiring combat engagement

danger_signals.visual_form: Environmental hazards integrated into stage design
Large distinctive enemy entities at stage conclusion
danger_signals.meaning: Critical threat blocking progression; requires defeat to advance
Special weapon unavailability forces reversion to default handgun; vulnerability state

danger_signals.visual_form: Player character damage state (no specific visual documented)
danger_signals.meaning: Proximity to mission failure; respawn/restart consequence

safety_signals.meaning: Health and ammunition restoration; safety recovery mechanic

safety_signals.visual_form: Special weapon items procured throughout stages
safety_signals.meaning: Tactical advantage; shotgun, machine gun, homing gun, laser rifle, grenade launcher availability

safety_signals.visual_form: Boss defeat transition to next stage
safety_signals.meaning: Mission progress; temporary respite before next threat

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Floppy disk (father's message and colony plans)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: US version: exposed face design; JP/EU versions: full-body armor covering head
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Space colony ruins, research facility remnants
Underground concourse, enemy hideout, flying spaceship, factory locations

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Large Sunsoft logo (unused Terminator copyright text in assets)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Title screen, regional title variants (Rough World JP, Journey to Silius international)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Title screen; protagonist standing against first enemy; floppy disk symbolic representation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player assumes control; prepares weapon systems for engagement

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Robotic enemies present; security systems visible; stage layout reveals terrorist infrastructure
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player navigates, shoots, switches weapons; enemies pursue and attack

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ruins environment stage 1; destroyed research facility aesthetic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Motivation narrative (avenging father); investigation of attack origin

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sequential environment shift: ruins → underground → hideout → spaceship → factory
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Infiltration deepens; player moves toward terrorist leadership; difficulty escalation implied

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Credits roll after final stage completion
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game cycles back to stage 1; cycle-based narrative loop (no permanent ending state documented)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Year 373 space age calendar; space colony #428 in Silius Solar System setting
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Justifies colonization mission; establishes protagonist's scientific heritage motivation

environmental_storytelling.example: Five distinct environments (ruins, underground, hideout, spaceship, factory) chart player's infiltration journey through terrorist organization
Progression from colony wreckage → hidden underground operations → central command structures suggests discovery arc
Five special weapons available throughout stages imply scavenged or distributed armaments within terrorist infrastructure
Stage-end bosses mark command hierarchy; defeating each represents dismantling organizational structure

environmental_storytelling.technique: Character design iteration as development narrative
environmental_storytelling.example: US version exposed-face design vs. JP/EU full-body armor visually communicates design evolution; signals armor upgrade storyline potential

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can lie down (tactical positioning); aims handgun/special weapons left or right; retrieves dropped capsules; switches weapon systems based on availability
Positioned throughout levels; pursue player; drop energy capsules upon defeat
Appear at stage conclusion; require focused combat engagement; defeat triggers stage transition
Integrated into environment; function as hazards; player must navigate around or through
Special weapons consume energy; depletion forces automatic reversion to default handgun; visual indicator of weapon viability

notes: Journey to Silius originated as a Terminator license game but pivoted to original IP during development. Manifestation patterns reflect this hybrid origin: mechanical enemies and industrial spaces echo Terminator aesthetic, but space-colony narrative and personal revenge motivation provide distinct identity. NES technical constraints limit visual communication to sprite-based enemies, environmental hazards, and UI gauge indicators. Regional differences (character armor design) suggest world-state variations across releases. No documented faction color-coding or detailed sigil system; threat identification relies on enemy type recognition and spatial positioning. Game cycle structure (restart after final stage) creates narrative loop without permanent resolution, implying ongoing terrorist threat despite mission completion. Gun Energy gauge serves as primary resource-scarcity signal. Environmental storytelling heavily dependent on stage sequencing rather than detailed background elements.
